title: Thinking Machines Lab Drops Its First Model
gist: Thinking Machines Lab, the OpenAI-defector startup led by Mira Murati that raised the largest seed round in history at a $12B valuation, released its first model, Inkling — a 975-billion-parameter open-weight multimodal (text/audio/video) model that isn't best-in-class on benchmarks but is claimed to rival top Chinese open-weight models, positioning the company as a legitimate open-source alternative in a market still led by closed labs like OpenAI and Anthropic.

insights.claim: Thinking Machines Lab released Inkling, its first model, as open-weight (downloadable and modifiable by researchers and startups), trained from scratch to handle audio, video, and text input, with 975 billion parameters requiring a cluster of specialized chips to run.
insights.speaker: Will Knight
insights.so_what: A 975B-parameter open-weight multimodal model is a substantial compute commitment; the requirement for a specialized chip cluster limits Inkling to well-resourced downstream users, not edge/consumer deployment.

insights.claim: Thinking Machines says Inkling is not the top performer on popular benchmarks but performs well across many tasks including advanced reasoning and coding, and claims performance comparable to the best open-weight models, which currently come from China.
insights.speaker: Will Knight
insights.so_what: Positions Inkling as a credible (if not leading) US-based challenger to Chinese open-weight models (e.g., DeepSeek-class releases), relevant to the geopolitical race for open-source AI model leadership.
insights.locator: It says that while Inkling isn't the best model

insights.claim: Thinking Machines used Inkling itself to fine-tune and improve the model, illustrating a growing industry pattern of AI models being used to build and refine other AI models.
insights.speaker: Will Knight
insights.so_what: Self-improving/self-tuning training loops are becoming standard practice, a trend relevant to compute-demand forecasts and the pace of capability iteration across AI labs.
insights.locator: In a sign of how AI models are increasingly being used

insights.claim: During training, Inkling's chain-of-thought reasoning explanations became more concise over time, dropping grammatical overhead while remaining comprehensible and without affecting the final response quality, according to Thinking Machines' blog post.
insights.speaker: Will Knight
insights.so_what: An emergent efficiency behavior in reasoning-model training (self-compressing chain-of-thought) is a notable technical data point for anyone tracking reasoning-model interpretability and inference-cost trends.
insights.quote: the chain of thought became more concise over time, dropping grammatical overhead while remaining comprehensible and leaving the final response unaffected
insights.locator: The training process also surfaced an interesting phenomenon

insights.claim: Thinking Machines has publicly argued AI technology should not be controlled by just a few companies and should be decentralized so more people can build their own models with their own data — a thesis the Inkling open-weight release is designed to embody.
insights.speaker: Will Knight
insights.so_what: An explicit decentralization thesis differentiates Thinking Machines from closed-model labs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) and aligns it with the open-weight camp (Meta Llama, Mistral, Chinese labs) — relevant for competitive-landscape mapping.
insights.locator: The release of an open-weight model fits with a vision

insights.claim: Releasing Inkling as open-weight could help Thinking Machines establish itself as a legitimate player in the AI race, since open-source models are popular for being cheaper to run than fee-based closed models and easier to modify for specific tasks.
insights.speaker: Will Knight
insights.so_what: Open-weight releases are a credibility and adoption strategy for challenger labs competing against incumbents with closed, monetized APIs.
insights.locator: The release could help Thinking Machines establish itself

insights.claim: Thinking Machines Lab was founded in February 2025 by ex-OpenAI leaders Mira Murati (former CTO and briefly CEO of OpenAI), John Schulman (OpenAI cofounder, key ChatGPT developer), and Lilian Weng (former OpenAI VP who led safety and robotics work), and raised the largest seed funding round in history, valuing the company at $12 billion at inception.
insights.speaker: Will Knight
insights.so_what: A $12B pre-product seed valuation for an OpenAI-defector team signals continued extreme capital concentration in AI talent/pedigree bets, a comparable data point for valuing other frontier-adjacent AI startups.
insights.locator: Thinking Machines was founded in February 2025

insights.claim: Prior to Inkling, Thinking Machines had released Tinker (a model fine-tuning tool), a natural voice-interaction tool, and published machine-learning research, establishing an infrastructure/tooling track record ahead of its first foundation model release.
insights.speaker: Will Knight
insights.so_what: The company's product sequencing (tools first, foundation model later) suggests a platform/ecosystem strategy rather than a pure model-race strategy, differentiating it from labs that lead with frontier model releases.

insights.claim: The article frames Thinking Machines and Anthropic as defector-led companies that have muscled into the AI space after OpenAI kick-started the boom with ChatGPT, noting Anthropic recently filed for an IPO that could value it at more than a trillion dollars.
insights.speaker: Will Knight
insights.so_what: A potential >$1 trillion Anthropic IPO valuation is a major signpost for AI-sector valuation benchmarks and public-market AI exposure, mentioned as context for Thinking Machines' competitive positioning.
insights.locator: OpenAI may have kick-started the AI boom
